subject: Mathematics
q: Considering only the principal values of inverse functions, the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: Considering only the principal values of inverse functions, the set <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced open="{" close="}" separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>≥</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mo>:</mo><mo> </mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
Given tan-12x+tan-13x=π4

⇒tan-12x+3x1-2x×3x=π4

Applying tangent on both sides,

⇒5x1-6x2=1 ∵tanπ4=1

⇒5x=1-6x2

⇒6x2+5x-1=0

⇒6x2+6x-x-1=0

⇒6x-1x+1=0

⇒x=-1, 16

But, since x≥0.

Hence, x=16

∴ A has only one element and thus, it is a singleton.

subject: Mathematics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>7</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>6</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>tan</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>3</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>7</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>6</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>tan</mi><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Given, sin-1sin2π3+cos-1cos7π6+tan-1tan3π4

Now simplifying each term we get, 

sin-1sin2π3=π-2π3=π3

cos-1cos7π6=2π-7π6=5π6

tan-1tan3π4=3π4-π=-π4

sin-1sin2π3+cos-1cos7π6+tan-1tan3π4

=π3+5π6-π4

=11π12

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\sin(\tan^{-1}(x\sqrt{2})) = \cot(\sin^{-1}\sqrt{1-x^2})$, $x \in (0,1)$, then the value of $x$ is :
question: If $\sin(\tan^{-1}(x\sqrt{2})) = \cot(\sin^{-1}\sqrt{1-x^2})$, $x \in (0,1)$, then the value of $x$ is :
solution:
Let $\alpha = \tan^{-1}(x\sqrt{2}) \Rightarrow \tan\alpha = x\sqrt{2}$

From the right-angled triangle, $\sin\alpha = \dfrac{x\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{1+2x^2}}$

Let $\beta = \sin^{-1}\sqrt{1-x^2} \Rightarrow \sin\beta = \sqrt{1-x^2}$

From the right-angled triangle, $\cos\beta = \sqrt{1 - (1-x^2)} = x$

$\cot\beta = \dfrac{\cos\beta}{\sin\beta} = \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}$

Given $\sin\alpha = \cot\beta$, substituting the values:

$\dfrac{x\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{1+2x^2}} = \dfrac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}$

Since $x \in (0,1)$, $x \neq 0$. Dividing by $x$ and squaring both sides:

$\dfrac{2}{1+2x^2} = \dfrac{1}{1-x^2}$

$2(1-x^2) = 1+2x^2$

$2 - 2x^2 = 1 + 2x^2$

$4x^2 = 1$

$x^2 = \dfrac{1}{4}$

Since $x \in (0,1)$, $x = \dfrac{1}{2}$

Answer: $\dfrac{1}{2}$

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⊂</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> be the largest interval for which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, holds . If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>a</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is equal to;
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>a</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>b</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>⊂</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> be the largest interval for which <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mi>θ</mi><mo>∈</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, holds . If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>10</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>b</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>a</mi></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> is equal to;
solution:
Given, sin-1sinθ-cos-1sinθ>0,θ∈0,2π

∵sin-1θ+cos-1θ=π2

∴sin-1sinθ-π2-sin-1sinθ>0

sin-1sinθ>π4⇒θ∈π4,3π4

a,b=π4,3π4⇒b-a=π2

Given b-a=α-β

∴ α-β=π2  .....(1)

Now αx2+βx+sin-1x2-6x+10+cos-1x2-6x+10=0

Now defining x2-6x+10=1+x-32≥1

Hence x=3 is the only possible solution

9α+3β+π2=0       …2

On solving equations 1 and 2 we get,

α=π12

q: The value of <math><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>12</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>13</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></math> is equal to:
question: The value of <math><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>12</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>13</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">sin</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mfrac><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>5</mn></mrow></mfrac></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow></mrow></mrow></math> is equal to:
solution:
sin-1⁡1213-sin-1⁡35
=tan-1⁡125-tan-1⁡34  sin-1⁡x=tan-1⁡x1-x2
=tan-1⁡125-341+12×35×4
=tan-1⁡3356
=sin-1⁡3365
=cos-1⁡5665=π2-sin-1⁡5665

q: For <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ℝ</mi></math>, let the solutions of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mi>cot</mi><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math>, where the inverse trigonometric functions take only principal values. If the solutions of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mi>β</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mi>β</mi></mfrac></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>b</mi><msup><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></math> is equal to ______.
question: For <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>k</mi><mo>∈</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">ℝ</mi></math>, let the solutions of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mi>cot</mi><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mi>k</mi><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mfenced close="|" open="|"><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo><</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math> be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>β</mi></math>, where the inverse trigonometric functions take only principal values. If the solutions of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>b</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> are <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mi>α</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac><mo>+</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msup><mi>β</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>α</mi><mi>β</mi></mfrac></math>, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>b</mi><msup><mi>k</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></mfrac></math> is equal to ______.
solution:
Given,

cossin-1xcottan-1cossin-1x=k

Now simplifying cossin-1x=coscos-11-x2=1-x2

So, cossin-1xcottan-1cossin-1x=k

becomes cossin-1xcottan-11-x2=k

And now solving  cottan-11-x2=cotcot-111-x2=11-x2

So, cossin-1xcottan-11-x2=k becomes 

cossin-1x1-x2=k

Now solving cossin-1x1-x2=1-2x21-x2

So, 1-2x21-x2=k

⇒1-2x2=k21-x2

⇒k2-2x2=k2-1

⇒x2=k2-1k2-2

So, roots are α=k2-1k2-2⇒α2=k2-1k2-2

And β=-k2-1k2-2⇒β2=k2-1k2-2

Now finding 1α2+1β2=2k2-2k2-1 and αβ=-1

So, sum of roots of x2-bx-5=0 will be =1α2+1β2+αβ=b

⇒2k2-2k2-1-1=b      ⋯1

Product of roots of x2-bx-5=0 will be =1α2+1β2αβ=-5

⇒2k2-2k2-1-1=-5

⇒2k2-4=5k2-5

⇒3k2=1⇒k2=13   ⋯Put in 1

⇒b=2k2-2k2-1-1=5-1=4

bk2=413=12

subject: Mathematics
q: Considering only the principal values of inverse trigonometric functions, the number of positive real values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> satisfying <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> is :
question: Considering only the principal values of inverse trigonometric functions, the number of positive real values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> satisfying <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>(</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>(</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>)</mo><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></math> is :
solution:
Let, 

fx=tan-1x+tan-12x

Now, differentiating the above function we get,

⇒f'x=11+x2+21+4x2

⇒f'x>0; ∀x∈R

⇒limx→∞fx=π and limx→-∞fx=-π

Hence, fx is monotonically increasing for x∈R and the range of fx is -π,π.

Hence, fx=π4 has exactly one solution for x>0.

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\dfrac{\pi}{4} + \displaystyle\sum_{p=1}^{11} \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2^{p-1}}{1 + 2^{2p-1}}\right) = \alpha$, then $\tan\alpha$ is equal to __________.
question: If $\dfrac{\pi}{4} + \displaystyle\sum_{p=1}^{11} \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2^{p-1}}{1 + 2^{2p-1}}\right) = \alpha$, then $\tan\alpha$ is equal to __________.
solution:
The general term of the given series is $T_p = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2^{p-1}}{1 + 2^{2p-1}}\right)$.

This can be rewritten by expressing the numerator and denominator in terms of $2^p$ and $2^{p-1}$:

$T_p = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2^p - 2^{p-1}}{1 + 2^p \cdot 2^{p-1}}\right)$

Using the inverse trigonometric identity $\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{x - y}{1 + xy}\right) = \tan^{-1}x - \tan^{-1}y$, we get:

$T_p = \tan^{-1}(2^p) - \tan^{-1}(2^{p-1})$

Summing $T_p$ from $p = 1$ to $11$ gives a telescoping series:

$\displaystyle\sum_{p=1}^{11} T_p = \left(\tan^{-1}(2^1) - \tan^{-1}(2^0)\right) + \left(\tan^{-1}(2^2) - \tan^{-1}(2^1)\right) + \dots + \left(\tan^{-1}(2^{11}) - \tan^{-1}(2^{10})\right)$

Canceling the intermediate terms, we obtain:

$\displaystyle\sum_{p=1}^{11} T_p = \tan^{-1}(2^{11}) - \tan^{-1}(2^0)$

Since $2^0 = 1$ and $\tan^{-1}(1) = \dfrac{\pi}{4}$, the sum becomes:

$\displaystyle\sum_{p=1}^{11} T_p = \tan^{-1}(2^{11}) - \dfrac{\pi}{4}$

Substituting this back into the expression for $\alpha$:

$\alpha = \dfrac{\pi}{4} + \tan^{-1}(2^{11}) - \dfrac{\pi}{4}$

$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(2^{11})$

Taking the tangent of both sides, we get:

$\tan\alpha = 2^{11} = 2048$

Answer: $2048$

subject: Mathematics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>cosec</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>cot</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mn>5</mn></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>cosec</mo><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>cot</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mn>5</mn></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>4</mn><mn>5</mn></mfrac></mfenced></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to:
solution:
Given, cosec2tan-115+tan-134

=cosectan-12×151-125+tan-134

=cosectan-1512+tan-134

=cosectan-1512+341-512×34

=cosectan-15633=6556

option 1

subject: Mathematics
q: Considering the principal values of the inverse trigonometric functions, the sum of all the solutions of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
question: Considering the principal values of the inverse trigonometric functions, the sum of all the solutions of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mi>x</mi></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> is equal to
solution:
Given,

cos-1x-2sin-1x=cos-12x

⇒cos-1x-2π2-cos-1x=cos-12x

⇒cos-1x-π+2cos-1x=cos-12x

⇒3cos-1x=π+cos-12x     ...1

Taking cos both side we get,

⇒cos3cos-1x=cosπ+cos-12x

⇒coscos-14x3-3x=-coscos-12x

⇒4x3-3x=-2x

⇒4x3=x

⇒x=0,±12

All will satisfy the original equation,

So, sum of all the solution will be 0+12-12=0

subject: Mathematics
q:
Let $S=\left\{\left(\begin{array}{ll}a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22}\end{array}\right): a_{i j} \in\{0,1,2\}, a_{11}=a_{22}\right\}$
Then the number of non-singular matrices in the set $S$ is :
question:
Let $S=\left\{\left(\begin{array}{ll}a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22}\end{array}\right): a_{i j} \in\{0,1,2\}, a_{11}=a_{22}\right\}$
Then the number of non-singular matrices in the set $S$ is :
solution:
The matrices in the form
$$
\left[\begin{array}{ll}
a_{11} & a_{12} \\
a_{21} & a_{22}
\end{array}\right], a_{i j} \in\{0,1,2\}, a_{11}=a_{12} \text { are }
$$
$$
\left[\begin{array}{cc}
0 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 / 1 / 2 & 0
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc}
1 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 / 1 / 2 & 1
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc}
2 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 / 1 / 2 & 2
\end{array}\right]
$$
At any place, $0 / 1 / 2$ means 0,1 or 2 will be the element at that place.

Hence there are total $27=3 \times 3+3 \times 3+3 \times 3$ ) matrices of the above form. Out of which the matrices which are singular are
$$
\left[\begin{array}{cc}
0 & 0 / 1 / 2 \\
0 & 0
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{cc}
0 & 0 \\
1 / 2 & 0
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{ll}
1 & 1 \\
1 & 1
\end{array}\right],\left[\begin{array}{ll}
2 & 2 \\
2 & 2
\end{array}\right]
$$
Hence there are total $7(=3+2+1+1)$ singular matrices.

Therefore number of all non-singular matrices in the given form $=27-7=20$

q: If y=∑k=16kcos-135coskx-45sinkx then dydx at x=0is
question: If y=∑k=16kcos-135coskx-45sinkx then dydx at x=0is
solution:
For, small values near 0, i.e., x<<..1

y=∑k=16kcos-1coskx·cosα-sinkx·sinα

=∑k=16kcos-1coskx+α
=∑k=16kkx+α

=∑k=16k2x+kα

 dydx=∑k=16k2=67136=91

subject: Mathematics
q: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>m</mi><mi>n</mi></mfrac><mo>(</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>n</mi></math> are co-prime natural numbers) be a solution of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>9</mn></mfrac></math> and let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>></mo><mi>β</mi><mo>)</mo></math> be the roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>n</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Then the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>)</mo></math> lies on the line
question: Let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>m</mi><mi>n</mi></mfrac><mo>(</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>n</mi></math> are co-prime natural numbers) be a solution of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mrow><mn>2</mn><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>9</mn></mfrac></math> and let <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>></mo><mi>β</mi><mo>)</mo></math> be the roots of the equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo>-</mo><mi>n</mi><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mi>m</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>n</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>. Then the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>(</mo><mi>α</mi><mo>,</mo><mi>β</mi><mo>)</mo></math> lies on the line
solution:
Given: cos2sin-1x=19

Let, sin-1x=θ

⇒cos2θ=19

⇒1-2sin2θ=19

⇒89=2sin2θ

⇒sinθ=±23

⇒x=±23

It is given that m and n are co-prime natural numbers,

⇒m=2, n=3

Also, mx2-nx-m+n=0.

⇒2x2-3x-2+3=0

⇒2x2-3x+1=0

⇒2x2-2x-x+1=0

⇒2xx-1-x-1=0

⇒2x-1x-1=0

⇒x=1,12

⇒α=1, β=12

Out of the given options, 1,12 lines on 5x+8y=9.

q: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mi>x</mi><mo><</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow><mi>α</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow><mi>β</mi></mfrac></math>, then a value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>α</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfrac></mfenced></math> is
question: If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo><</mo><mi>x</mi><mo><</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><msqrt><mn>2</mn></msqrt></mfrac></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mrow><msup><mi>sin</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow><mi>α</mi></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><msup><mi>cos</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mi>x</mi></mrow><mi>β</mi></mfrac></math>, then a value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>π</mi><mi>α</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>α</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>β</mi></mrow></mfrac></mfenced></math> is
solution:
Let sin-1xα=cos-1xβ=k

i.e. sin-1x=kα and cos-1x=kβ

⇒sin-1x+cos-1x=kα+β

i.e. kα+β=π2

⇒k=π2α+β

Now 2παα+β=4kα=4sin-1x

So sin2παα+β=sin4sin-1x

=2sin2sin-1xcos2sin-1x

=2sinsin-12x1-x2cossin-12x1-x2

=2sinsin-12x1-x2coscos-11-2x2

=4x1-x21-2x2

subject: Mathematics
q: Using the principal values of the inverse trigonometric functions, the sum of the maximum and the minimum values of $16\left(\left(\sec ^{-1} x\right)^2+\left(\operatorname{cosec}^{-1} x\right)^2\right)$ is :
question: Using the principal values of the inverse trigonometric functions, the sum of the maximum and the minimum values of $16\left(\left(\sec ^{-1} x\right)^2+\left(\operatorname{cosec}^{-1} x\right)^2\right)$ is :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & 16\left(\sec ^{-1} x\right)^2+\left(\operatorname{cosec}^{-1} x\right)^2 \\ & \operatorname{Sec}^{-1} x=a \in[0, \pi]-\left\{\frac{\pi}{2}\right\} \\ & \operatorname{cosec}^{-1} x=\frac{\pi}{2}-a \\ & =16\left[a^2+\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-a\right)^2\right]=16\left[2 a^2-\pi a+\frac{\pi^2}{4}\right] \\ & \max ]_{a=\pi}=16\left[2 \pi^2-\pi^2+\pi \frac{2}{4}\right]=20 \pi^2 \\ & \min ]_{a=\frac{\pi}{4}}=16\left[\frac{2 \times \pi^2}{16}-\frac{\pi^2}{4}+\frac{\pi^2}{4}\right]=2 \pi^2 \\ & \operatorname{Sum}=22 \pi^2\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: If $\alpha\gt\beta\gt\gamma\gt0$, then the expression $\cot ^{-1}\left\{\beta+\frac{\left(1+\beta^2\right)}{(\alpha-\beta)}\right\}+\cot ^{-1}\left\{\gamma+\frac{\left(1+\gamma^2\right)}{(\beta-\gamma)}\right\}+\cot ^{-1}\left\{\alpha+\frac{\left(1+\alpha^2\right)}{(\gamma-\alpha)}\right\}$ is equal to :
question: If $\alpha\gt\beta\gt\gamma\gt0$, then the expression $\cot ^{-1}\left\{\beta+\frac{\left(1+\beta^2\right)}{(\alpha-\beta)}\right\}+\cot ^{-1}\left\{\gamma+\frac{\left(1+\gamma^2\right)}{(\beta-\gamma)}\right\}+\cot ^{-1}\left\{\alpha+\frac{\left(1+\alpha^2\right)}{(\gamma-\alpha)}\right\}$ is equal to :
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{\alpha \beta+1}{\alpha-\beta}\right)+\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{\beta \gamma+1}{\beta-\gamma}\right)+\cot ^{-1}\left(\frac{\alpha \gamma+1}{\gamma-\alpha}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\alpha-\beta}{1+\alpha \beta}\right)+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\beta-\gamma}{1+\beta \gamma}\right)+\pi+\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\gamma-\alpha}{1+\gamma \alpha}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow\left(\tan ^{-1} \alpha-\tan ^{-1} \beta\right)+\left(\tan ^{-1} \beta-\tan ^{-1} \gamma\right)+\left(\pi+\tan ^{-1} \gamma-\tan ^{-1} \alpha\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \pi\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
q: The sum of possible values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cot</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>8</mn><mn>31</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> is:
question: The sum of possible values of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> for <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>+</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>+</mo><msup><mi>cot</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfrac></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced><mfrac><mn>8</mn><mn>31</mn></mfrac></mfenced></math> is:
solution:
Given tan-1x+1+cot-11x-1=tan-1831

⇒tan-1x+1+tan-1x-1=tan-1831,   ∴tan-1x=cot-11x, x>0

⇒tan-1x+1+x-11-x+1x-1=tan-1831

⇒tan-12x1-x+1x-1=tan-1831

Taking tangent both the sides we get, 

(x+1)+(x-1)1-x2-1=831

⇒2x2-x2=831

⇒4x2+31x-8=0

⇒x=-8,14

But, if x=14

tan-1x+1∈0,π2

and cot-11x-1∈π2,π

⇒LHS>π2 and RHS<π2 (Not possible)

Hence, x=-8=-324.

q: The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>tan</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mfrac><mrow><mi>cos</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mrow><mn>15</mn><mi>π</mi></mrow><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mfenced><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mn>4</mn></mfrac></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></mfenced></math> is equal to

solution:
Given,

 tan-1cos15π4-1sinπ4

=tan-1cos4π-π4-1sinπ4

=tan-112-112

=tan-11-2 =-tan-12-1

=-π8

subject: Mathematics
q: If $k=\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)\right)+\tan \left(\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)\right)$, then the number of solutions of the equation $\sin ^{-1}(k x-1)=\sin ^{-1} x-\cos ^{-1} x$ is $\_\_\_\_$
question: If $k=\tan \left(\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{1}{2} \cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)\right)+\tan \left(\frac{1}{2} \sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{3}\right)\right)$, then the number of solutions of the equation $\sin ^{-1}(k x-1)=\sin ^{-1} x-\cos ^{-1} x$ is $\_\_\_\_$
solution:
Let $\theta = \dfrac{1}{2} \sin^{-1} \dfrac{2}{3}$, then $\dfrac{1}{2} \cos^{-1} \dfrac{1}{3} = \left( \dfrac{\pi}{4} - \theta \right)$
$k = \tan\theta + \cot\theta = \dfrac{1}{\sin\theta \cos\theta} = \dfrac{2}{\sin 2\theta}$
$k = \dfrac{2}{\dfrac{2}{3}} = 3$
$\sin^{-1}(3x - 1) = \sin^{-1}x - \cos^{-1}x$
$\sin^{-1}(3x - 1) = \dfrac{\pi}{2} - 2\cos^{-1}x$
$3x - 1 = \sin\left( \dfrac{\pi}{2} - 2\cos^{-1}x \right)$
$3x - 1 = 2x^2 - 1 \Rightarrow x = 0, \dfrac{3}{2}$ (rejected)
No. of solution $= 1$
